text: The surgeon lifts the mediastinal pleura to identify the right vagus nerve in the infra-azygous compartment, preserving the bronchial branch of the vagus nerve. The azygos vein is held up and dissected around, then clipped on both sides with hemolog clips and cut using laparoscopic scissors.

The surgeon identifies the right vagus nerve in the suprazygous portion and carefully cuts the first layer of the mediastinal pleura, revealing the right recurrent laryngeal nerve hooking around the right subclavian artery. The mediastinal pleura is lifted and incised up to the thoracic inlet, and the right recurrent laryngeal nerve is pushed down while fibro fatty tissue, lymphatics, and lymph nodal basins are lifted into the specimen.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the right recurrent laryngeal nerve, avoiding high-energy sources to prevent thermal injury. Scissors are used to lift fibro fatty and lymphatic tissue off the nerve, preserving the main branch that supplies the vocal cords. The recurrent laryngeal nerve is seen hooking around the pulsating right subclavian artery, while smaller nerve branches supplying the trachea are safely cut. The dissection continues along the lateral border of the trachea, pushing down the recurrent laryngeal nerve as lymphatics and adhesions are released.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the recurrent laryngeal nerve, cutting small nerve branches that supply the trachea and esophagus. The dissection proceeds along the lateral border of the trachea and the medial border of the recurrent laryngeal nerve towards the thoracic inlet. The recurrent laryngeal nerve is visualized from its origin at the subclavian artery to the thoracic inlet, with the main branch pushed away from the esophagus and trachea. Fibro fatty tissue and lymph nodal basins are lifted off the lateral wall of the trachea, revealing the right inferior thyroid artery in close relation to the recurrent laryngeal nerve.

That brings us to the five easy steps of how a recurrent laryngeal nerve dissection should be done. At the end of the procedure, it is important to have hemostasis. We usually use Evacil to bring hemostasis for small bleeding points, which are sprayed on at the end of the procedure. Let's revise the anatomy. That is the recurrent arangeal nerve taking off from the right vagus. The right recurrent arangeal nerve goes all above, all along the lateral border of the trachea. Here it hooks around the subclavian artery and goes cranially towards the neck to get inserted into the vocal cords. So right recurrent laryngeal nerve dissection is also called as suprazygous mediastinal lymphadenectomy. It is also called as extended two-field lymphadenectomy. It is important to know that any thoracoscopic esophagectomy is a team effort and we from Max Institute of Cancer Care Delhi bring this video to you. Thank you.
